questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem:
Passage: Underground mining is one of the most hazardous occupations in the world. The primary risks include roof falls, gas explosions, flooding, and ventilation failures. In India, the Mines Act of 1952 and the Coal Mines Regulations of 2017 govern safety standards. Modern technology has significantly improved mine safety through real-time monitoring systems, gas detectors, and automated equipment. Despite these advances, safety remains a critical concern, and regular training programs are mandatory for all mine workers. The introduction of continuous miners and longwall mining methods has increased both productivity and safety.

Continuous miners and longwall mining methods have increased:

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Meaning of 'To get down to brass tacks':

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem:
Passage: Coal is the most abundant fossil fuel in India and plays a crucial role in the country's energy mix. India has the fourth-largest coal reserves in the world, with an estimated 344 billion tonnes. Coal India Limited (CIL) is the largest coal-producing company globally, accounting for about 82% of India's coal output. However, concerns about environmental degradation and climate change have prompted a shift towards cleaner energy sources like solar and wind power. The government has set a target of achieving 500 GW of renewable energy capacity by 2030.

What percentage of India's coal output does CIL account for?

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Meaning of 'To turn a blind eye':

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem:
Passage: The Gondwana coal fields of eastern India contain the country's best quality coal. These fields stretch across Jharkhand, West Bengal, Odisha, and Chhattisgarh. The coal found here is primarily bituminous, which has high calorific value and low ash content. In contrast, the Tertiary coal fields of northeastern India contain lignite and sub-bituminous coal. Coal grading is based on ash content, with higher grades having lower ash percentages. Washeries are used to reduce ash content and improve coal quality before it is supplied to power plants and industries.

Coal grading is primarily based on:

Despite these advances, safety remains a critical concern, and regular training programs are mandatory for all mine workers. The introduction of continuous miners and longwall mining methods has increased both productivity and safety.

Which of the following is NOT mentioned as a risk in underground mining?

questions.stem: Meaning of 'To play devil's advocate':
options: To argue for the opposite side

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem:
Passage: The Industrial Revolution, which began in Britain in the late 18th century, was powered largely by coal. Steam engines, fuelled by coal, drove factories, railways, and ships, transforming economies and societies. The demand for coal led to the expansion of mining operations across Europe and America. However, the social cost was high, with poor working conditions, child labour, and environmental pollution. The legacy of the Industrial Revolution continues to influence modern industrial practices and environmental policies. Today, many former mining regions are being revitalised through diversification and clean energy initiatives.

Former mining regions are being revitalised through:

questions.stem: Meaning of 'To go the extra mile':

The coal found here is primarily bituminous, which has high calorific value and low ash content. In contrast, the Tertiary coal fields of northeastern India contain lignite and sub-bituminous coal. Coal grading is based on ash content, with higher grades having lower ash percentages. Washeries are used to reduce ash content and improve coal quality before it is supplied to power plants and industries.

Washeries are used to:

options: To be in the same situation

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem:
Passage: Underground mining is one of the most hazardous occupations in the world. The primary risks include roof falls, gas explosions, flooding, and ventilation failures. In India, the Mines Act of 1952 and the Coal Mines Regulations of 2017 govern safety standards. Modern technology has significantly improved mine safety through real-time monitoring systems, gas detectors, and automated equipment. Despite these advances, safety remains a critical concern, and regular training programs are mandatory for all mine workers. The introduction of continuous miners and longwall mining methods has increased both productivity and safety.

What has improved mine safety according to the passage?
options: Real-time monitoring systems and gas detectors
questions.solution: Real-time monitoring systems and gas detectors

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem:
Passage: The Industrial Revolution, which began in Britain in the late 18th century, was powered largely by coal. Steam engines, fuelled by coal, drove factories, railways, and ships, transforming economies and societies. The demand for coal led to the expansion of mining operations across Europe and America. However, the social cost was high, with poor working conditions, child labour, and environmental pollution. The legacy of the Industrial Revolution continues to influence modern industrial practices and environmental policies. Today, many former mining regions are being revitalised through diversification and clean energy initiatives.

The Industrial Revolution began in which country?

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Rearrange: P: Renewable energy sources are gaining importance. Q: Solar and wind power are the most promising. R: Their cost has decreased dramatically. S: Many countries have set ambitious renewable energy targets.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Rearrange: monitoring / technology / improved / has / safety / mine
options: Improved technology has mine safety monitoring
Monitoring mine safety has technology improved
Technology has improved mine safety monitoring
Mine safety monitoring technology has improved
questions.solution: Technology has improved mine safety monitoring

questions.stem: Annual demand = 1000 units, ordering cost = ₹100/order, holding cost = ₹8/unit/yr. The EOQ is about:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Component A (R = 0.9) is in series with a parallel pair B (0.85) and C (0.9). The system reliability is about:

questions.subject: Industrial Engineering
questions.stem: Mager and Pipe performance model:
options: Discriminates between skill, knowledge, and environmental causes
questions.solution: Can they do it? Will they do it? Is it worth it?

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: EOQ: D=15000/yr, S=$60, H=$3/unit/yr. Q*=

questions.subject: Industrial Engineering
questions.stem: Reliability growth model: Duane model assumes:
options: Cumulative MTBF improves as power function of time
questions.solution: Duane: MTBF_cumulative = aT^b. Log-linear relationship. b is growth rate. Used during development testing.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: X-bar chart: X̄=25, R̄=2, A₂=0.577. UCL≈

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Function cost matrix (VE):
options: Functions vs component cost. High cost / low worth = target. Function = verb + noun
questions.solution: Function cost matrix: heat map of cost per function. Identify overdesigned components.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Simulation — types and output analysis:
options: Discrete event (DES), continuous (system dynamics), agent-based. Steady-state vs terminating. Warm-up period
questions.solution: DES: process interactions. ARENA, AnyLogic. Output analysis: Welch for warm-up, batch means for CI.

questions.subject: Industrial Engineering
questions.stem: 5 Why analysis:
options: Root cause finding by asking why repeatedly
questions.solution: 5 Whys: drill down to root cause.

questions.subject: Industrial Engineering
questions.stem: Trace in simulation: provides:
questions.solution: Trace: detailed log of each event, entity, variable change. Essential for debugging. Can be very long.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Work study: method study vs work measurement:
options: Method study: how to do the job (improve method). Work measurement: how long it takes (set time)
questions.solution: Method study: systematic examination. Work measurement: determine standard time. Together: work study.

questions.subject: Industrial Engineering
questions.stem: Lagrangian relaxation: moves complicating constraints to:
questions.solution: LR: relax hard constraints into objective with multipliers. Decomposes problem. Provides bounds for B&B.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Consignment inventory:
options: Supplier-owned inventory at buyer location. Payment when consumed. Buyer reduces risk, supplier finances
questions.solution: Consignment: common in retail (books, electronics). VMI often combined. Risks: obsolescence on supplier.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Design for assembly (DFA):
options: Reduce assembly operations. Symmetrical parts. Snap-fit instead of fasteners
questions.solution: DFA: self-locating, self-fastening. Top-down assembly. Modular subassemblies.

questions.subject: Industrial Engineering
questions.stem: Fixed-position layout is used when:
options: Product is too large to move (ships, buildings)
questions.solution: Fixed-position: product stays, workers/equipment come to it. Used for large projects. Space planning critical.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Advanced MRP (closed-loop MRP):
options: MPS → RCCP → MRP → CRP → Shop floor control. Feedback loop from SFC to MRP/MPS
questions.solution: Closed-loop: capacity plan feasible. Reschedule: firm planned orders (FPO). Pegging.

questions.subject: Industrial Engineering
questions.stem: Preventive maintenance: optimal interval is when:
options: Marginal cost of PM = marginal failure risk cost
questions.solution: PM optimization: balance PM cost against failure cost. Too frequent: unnecessary PM cost. Too infrequent: failure cost.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For the CPM network shown (path 1-2-4 = A+B = 3+7, path 1-3-4 = C+D = 3+5), the project duration (critical path) is:
questions.solution: Path durations: 1-2-4 = 10, 1-3-4 = 8. The critical path is the longest = 10 days.

questions.subject: Industrial Engineering
questions.stem: Accommodation range of eye is maximum when:
questions.solution: Accommodation: ability to focus at different distances. Decreases with age. Near point recedes. By 45-50, reading glasses needed.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Decision — expected value with perfect information (EVPI):
options: EVPI = EVwPI - EVwoPI. Value of perfect information
questions.solution: EVPI: maximum a decision maker would pay for perfect info. Sensitivity analysis.

questions.subject: Industrial Engineering
questions.stem: Lean supply chain principles include:
options: Pull-based demand, continuous flow, waste elimination
questions.solution: Lean: pull, flow, eliminate muda. Just-in-time.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: ANOVA for GRR:
options: Two-way: part × operator + interaction. σ²_gage = σ²_repeat + σ²_reprod. %GRR = σ_gage/σ_total × 100
questions.solution: ANOVA: part (df = n-1), operator (df = o-1), interaction (df = (n-1)(o-1)), error (df = n·o·(r-1)).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: An assembly line has a cycle time of 2 min and runs for 480 min/shift. The shift output is:
questions.solution: Output = available time / cycle time = 480/2 = 240 units.

questions.subject: Industrial Engineering
questions.stem: PERT: expected time = (O + 4M + P) / 6:
questions.solution: PERT weighted average: optimistic, most likely (4×), pessimistic. Assumes beta distribution. Used for activity durations.

questions.subject: Industrial Engineering
questions.stem: Lean Manufacturing: 7 wastes (Muda) include:
options: Transport, Inventory, Motion, Waiting, Over-processing, Overproduction, Defects
questions.solution: TIM WOOD: Transport, Inventory, Motion, Waiting, Over-processing, Overproduction, Defects. 8th waste: Skills not used.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: On the break-even chart shown (fixed cost ₹20000, price ₹80, variable cost ₹40/unit), the marked point where revenue meets total cost occurs at:
questions.solution: BEP = FC/(p − v) = 20000/(80 − 40) = 500 units, where total revenue = total cost.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For the CPM network shown (path 1-2-4 = A+B = 5+7, path 1-3-4 = C+D = 3+10), the project duration (critical path) is:
questions.solution: Path durations: 1-2-4 = 12, 1-3-4 = 13. The critical path is the longest = 13 days.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Control chart — c chart:
options: Number of defects per unit (constant sample size). Poisson-based
questions.solution: c-chart: UCL = c̄ + 3√c̄. LCL = c̄ - 3√c̄. For discrete defects (e.g., scratches per m²).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge

options: Receiving doors on one side, shipping on other. Sort items directly across

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: On the break-even chart shown (fixed cost ₹10000, price ₹80, variable cost ₹40/unit), the marked point where revenue meets total cost occurs at:
questions.solution: BEP = FC/(p − v) = 10000/(80 − 40) = 250 units, where total revenue = total cost.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Scheduling:
options: Timing of operations. Gantt chart, Johnson's rule (2-machine flow shop)
questions.solution: Scheduling: sequencing + timing. Gantt chart: visual. Johnson: minimize makespan.

questions.subject: Industrial Engineering
questions.stem: Integer programming branch and bound prunes branch when:
options: Lower bound exceeds current best integer solution
questions.solution: B&B: if bound worse than best known, prune.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Standard time calculation: ST = NT * (1 + allowance). If allowance is 20%:
questions.solution: Allowance: added to normal time. 20% allowance: ST = NT * 1.20. Personal, fatigue, delay.

options: λ = mean demand rate/yr. P(x) = e^{-λ}λ^x/x!. Stockout probability for given stock level
questions.solution: Poisson: for low demand items (λ < 10). Fill rate calculation. Multi-item optimization.

options: Optimal location minimizing weighted distance. x̄ = Σd_ix·W_i/ΣW_i
questions.solution: Center of gravity: coordinates weighted by volume. For distribution centers.

questions.stem: Inventory pooling — risk pooling effect:
options: Centralized inventory → lower safety stock for same service level. Square root law: SS_central = SS_decentralized × √n
questions.solution: Square root law: n DCs → √n × SS. Consider demand correlation between locations.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: ABC analysis classifies inventory items by their:
questions.solution: 'A' items have high value and tight control; 'C' items are low value with loose control.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Which statement about the critical path is INCORRECT?
options: It is the shortest path through the network
It is the longest path through the network
It determines the minimum project duration
questions.solution: The critical path is the LONGEST path; calling it the shortest is wrong.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge

questions.solution: CRP: compares load vs available capacity. Overtime, shift, subcontract. Relief by reschedule.

questions.solution: DBR: bottleneck controls pace. Time buffer before bottleneck. Rope releases material at bottleneck rate.

options: JIT reduces inventory by producing to actual demand
questions.solution: JIT pulls production to demand with small lots, cutting inventory and exposing waste.

questions.stem: A product is used at 20 units/day with a lead time of 10 days. The reorder point (no safety stock) is:
questions.solution: ROP = demand rate × lead time = 20×10 = 200 units.

questions.solution: ROP = demand rate × lead time = 80×5 = 400 units.

options: Loss per unit over-ordered. c-s (cost - salvage value)
questions.solution: Newsvendor: if Cu > Co → SL > 0.5. Perishable goods, seasonal fashion, holiday items.

questions.subject: Industrial Engineering
questions.stem: Frozen period in MPS:
options: Cannot be changed without management approval
questions.solution: Frozen: lock period for stability. Master scheduler cannot change without VP approval. Provides stability for execution.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: On the control chart shown (CL = 50, UCL = 59, LCL = 41), the highlighted point lying above the UCL indicates that the process is:
options: In control with only chance variation
Out of control (an assignable cause is present)
questions.solution: A point beyond the ±3σ control limits (UCL = 59) signals an assignable cause — the process is out of statistical control.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Bias in forecast: consistently over or under forecasting. Detected by:
options: Mean Error (ME) ≠ 0 or Tracking Signal outside control limits
questions.solution: ME = Σe_t/n. Positive: underforecast. Negative: overforecast. Should be close to zero.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Holt-Winters — additive seasonality:
options: Level + trend + seasonal. S = seasonal component. L_t = α(D_t - S_{t-s}) + (1-α)(L_{t-1}+T_{t-1})
questions.solution: Additive: when seasonal amplitude constant. Multiplicative: when seasonal amplitude proportional to level.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: LP — unbounded solution:
options: Objective unlimited. Feasible region open. No finite optimum
questions.solution: Unbounded: missing constraint. Check entering variable has no positive ratio.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: CDS algorithm (Campbell):
options: Extends Johnson to m machines. k = m-1 subproblems
questions.solution: CDS: create 2-machine problems (machine 1 to k vs machine m-k+1 to m). Pick best makespan.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Cyclegraph / chronocyclegraph:
options: Light traces of worker movements. 3D path recording. Flash rate for velocity
questions.solution: Cyclegraph: continuous path. Chronocyclegraph: interrupted (dash) for speed/direction.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Forecast error measures:
options: MAD = Σ|e|/n, MSE = Σe²/n, MAPE = Σ|e/D|/n × 100
questions.solution: MAD: mean absolute deviation. MSE: penalizes large errors. MAPE: percentage error.
